title: Veterans 2nd Amendment Restoration Act of 2025
plain_summary: This bill is about veterans and their right to buy guns. Right now, if the Veterans Affairs Department decides a veteran needs someone else to help manage their benefits money, that information gets shared with the background check system and can stop the veteran from buying guns. This bill says that needing help with benefits alone should not prevent veterans from buying guns. The Veterans Affairs Department must tell the Justice Department that it was wrong to share this kind of information about veterans since 1993.
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Disability Assistance and Memorial Affairs.

sections.title: Section 1  Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Veterans 2nd Amendment Restoration Act of 2025.

sections.title: Section 2  Notification of Lack of Basis for the Secretary of Veterans Affairs to Have Transmitted Certain Information to the Department of Justice for Use by the National Instant Criminal Background Check System
sections.text: The Secretary of Veterans Affairs must notify the Attorney General within thirty days after this law is passed. The Secretary must tell the Attorney General that sharing certain veteran information was wrong. Since November thirtieth nineteen ninety three, the Veterans Affairs Department has been sending personal information about veterans to the Justice Department. This information was sent only because the Veterans Affairs Department decided that a veteran needed someone else to handle their benefit payments for them. This personal information was then used in the national instant criminal background check system that gun stores use when people try to buy guns. The Secretary must now tell the Attorney General that sharing this information was improper and should not have happened or no longer applies.

sections.title: Section 3  Determination by the Secretary of Veterans Affairs That a Person Is Mentally Incompetent Is Insufficient to Treat Such Person as a Mental Defective for Certain Purposes Regarding Firearms or Ammunition
sections.text: Under federal gun laws, a person cannot be considered mentally defective just because the Veterans Affairs Department made certain decisions about them. If the Veterans Affairs Department decided that a veteran is mentally incompetent under their regulations or that the veteran needs someone else to handle their benefit payments, this alone cannot be used to stop that person from buying or owning guns or ammunition under federal law.
